title: Hokkaido milk bread
subtitle: Soft, pillowy enriched white loaf made with the tangzhong method
excerpt: A Japanese-style enriched white loaf made exceptionally soft by the tangzhong method: a small amount of flour is cooked with milk into a paste before being folded into the main dough. The result is a feather-light crumb that stays soft for days. Makes one 900 g loaf.
sourceNotes: Hokkaido milk bread (shokupan-style) is a twentieth-century Japanese enriched bread developed from Western pullman loaf techniques, made distinct by the tangzhong (water roux) method. The tangzhong technique is documented in Yvonne Chen, The 65°C Bread Doctor (2004), which established the 65°C water roux for Asian-style enriched breads. The recipe is synthesised from this tradition and from general bread-science principles (gelatinisation of starch at 65°C trapping additional moisture) documented in food science literature in the public domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze in a bag for up to 1 month. Slices toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best on the day it is baked, but stays soft for 2 days in a sealed bag.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the loaf is ready when deep golden on top and sounds hollow when tapped on the base

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked flour-and-liquid paste used in Japanese enriched bread. A small portion of the total flour (around 10%) is stirred into milk or water over low heat until the mixture reaches 65°C and thickens to a smooth paste. At this temperature the starch gelatinises and can hold roughly five times its weight in water; this trapped moisture transfers to the crumb during baking and keeps the loaf soft for days. The paste is cooled before being added to the main dough.

content.text: is made first and must cool completely before it goes into the dough; hot paste will kill the yeast. The dough is enriched with butter, eggs, and sugar, which slow the gluten development: give it the full kneading time regardless of how it looks at the 5-minute mark.

items.prepNote: beaten with 1 tbsp milk, for glazing

content.text: Whisk the {{plain-flour}} and {{whole-milk}} (the tangzhong portion) together in a small saucepan. Stir over low heat until the mixture thickens to a smooth paste that holds its shape when you run a line through it with the spoon; about 3 to 4 minutes. Transfer to a bowl and leave to cool completely.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Add the cooled tangzhong, the warm {{whole-milk}} (dough portion), and the {{eggs}}. Mix to a shaggy dough, then turn out and knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} a few cubes at a time, kneading after each addition until fully incorporated. The dough will be soft and slightly tacky; continue for 2 minutes until it pulls away from your hands cleanly.
Shape the dough into a ball, place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until roughly doubled.

Grease a 2 lb loaf tin. Turn the dough out and divide into 4 equal pieces. Shape each piece into a ball, then roll each ball flat and fold in thirds like a letter. Place seam-side down in the tin in a row. Cover loosely and prove for 60 minutes at room temperature until the dough crowns about 1 cm above the rim. Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Brush the surface gently with the {{eggs}} glaze and bake for 30 to 35 minutes until deep golden. Turn out onto a rack and cool for at least 30 minutes before slicing.
